description: The Fight for $15 movement, launched by fast-food workers in 2012, gradually shifted public opinion on a $15 federal minimum wage from ~50% support (2014) to ~67% (2019). City and state-level victories created demonstration effects that reduced opposition over five years.

key_events.description: Fight for $15 movement gains momentum (2014). Fast-food strikes in 150+ cities. Seattle becomes first major city to pass $15 minimum wage. Issue enters mainstream debate.
New York and California announce phased $15 minimum wage laws (2016). 'Sky didn't fall' narrative from Seattle early data. Democrats adopt $15 as platform plank.
Amazon raises minimum wage to $15 (October 2018). Jeff Bezos challenges other retailers. Target, Costco follow. Corporate adoption legitimizes the number.
House passes Raise the Wage Act (July 2019) with $15 target. CBO estimates 1.3M lifted from poverty but 1.3M job losses. Debate shifts from 'if' to 'how fast.'
2019 polls: Pew shows 67% support raising to $15. Even 43% of Republicans support $12+. Florida votes 60.8% for $15 ballot measure in 2020. Overton window shifted.

calibration_notes: Textbook gradual opinion shift driven by demonstration effects and Overton window movement. Pew Research tracked support growing steadily: 2014 (~50%), 2016 (~58%), 2019 (~67%). Key mechanism: city/state-level policy adoption provided evidence that predicted job losses didn't materialize at predicted scale, converting undecideds. Corporate adoption (Amazon, Target) further normalized the $15 figure. Tests: slow consensus building, demonstration effect, Overton window shift, bottom-up to mainstream trajectory.
